965	/-- **L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence** (`lem:gf_generic_rank_ses`,
966	Nitsure §4 inductive step). For `A` a noetherian domain and `d ≥ 0`, a finite
967	module `N` over the polynomial ring `P_d := A[X_1,…,X_d]`, there exist `m ∈ ℕ`
968	and an injective `P_d`-linear map `φ : P_d^{⊕m} → N` whose cokernel
969	`T := N ⧸ range φ` is a torsion `P_d`-module. Equivalently, a short exact
970	sequence `0 → P_d^{⊕m} → N → T → 0` with `T` torsion.
971	
972	`m` is the generic rank: `dim_{Frac P_d} (Frac P_d ⊗_{P_d} N)`. The map `φ`
973	is the `P_d`-linear combination of `m` lifts `v : Fin m → N` whose images form a
974	`Frac P_d`-basis of the localisation `N_Q := LocalizedModule (P_d)⁰ N`. This step
975	is built over `P_d` directly — no inversion of any `g ∈ A` is required. -/
976	theorem gf_generic_rank_ses
977	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
978	    (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
979	    [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
980	    [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
981	    ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
982	        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
983	      Function.Injective φ ∧
984	      Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ) := by
985	  classical
986	  -- Notation: `P = A[X_1,…,X_d]`, its non-zero divisors `S`, fraction field `K`,
987	  -- localisation `NK = N_Q`, and the localisation map `ℓ : N → NK`.
988	  let P := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A
989	  let S := nonZeroDivisors P
990	  let K := Localization S
991	  let NK := LocalizedModule S N
992	  let ℓ : N →ₗ[P] NK := LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap S N
993	  -- `NK` is a finite-dimensional `K`-vector space; take a basis indexed by `Fin m`.
994	  let m := Module.finrank K NK
995	  let b : Module.Basis (Fin m) K NK := Module.finBasis K NK
996	  -- Lift each basis vector along `ℓ`, clearing its denominator.
997	  obtain ⟨lift, hlift⟩ :
998	      ∃ lift : Fin m → N × S, ∀ i, ((lift i).2 : P) • b i = ℓ (lift i).1 := by
999	    refine ⟨fun i => (IsLocalizedModule.surj S ℓ (b i)).choose, fun i => ?_⟩
1000	    exact (IsLocalizedModule.surj S ℓ (b i)).choose_spec
1001	  let v : Fin m → N := fun i => (lift i).1
1002	  -- The denominators map to units of `K`.
1003	  let u : Fin m → Kˣ := fun i => (IsLocalization.map_units K (lift i).2).unit
1004	  have hlv : ∀ i, ℓ (v i) = (↑(u i) : K) • b i := by
1005	    intro i
1006	    have hu : (↑(u i) : K) = algebraMap P K ((lift i).2 : P) :=
1007	      (IsLocalization.map_units K (lift i).2).unit_spec
1008	    rw [hu, algebraMap_smul]
1009	    exact (hlift i).symm
1010	  -- The images `ℓ (v i)` are linearly independent over `K` (unit multiples of a basis).
1011	  have hLIK : LinearIndependent K (fun i => ℓ (v i)) := by
1012	    have h := b.linearIndependent.units_smul u
1013	    have heq : (fun i => ℓ (v i)) = (u • (⇑b)) := by
1014	      funext i; rw [hlv i]; rfl
1015	    rw [heq]; exact h
1016	  -- Restrict scalars to `P` (the inclusion `P → K` is injective), then descend to `v`.
1017	  have hLIP : LinearIndependent P (fun i => ℓ (v i)) := by
1018	    refine hLIK.restrict_scalars ?_
1019	    intro x y hxy
1020	    apply IsFractionRing.injective P K
1021	    rw [Algebra.algebraMap_eq_smul_one, Algebra.algebraMap_eq_smul_one]
1022	    exact hxy
1023	  have hLIv : LinearIndependent P v := LinearIndependent.of_comp ℓ hLIP
1024	  -- The map `φ : P^{⊕m} → N`, `e_i ↦ v i`.
1025	  refine ⟨m, Fintype.linearCombination P v, ?_, ?_⟩
1026	  · -- Injectivity of `φ` is exactly linear independence of `v`.
1027	    rw [← LinearMap.ker_eq_bot, LinearMap.ker_eq_bot']
1028	    intro g hg
1029	    rw [Fintype.linearCombination_apply] at hg
1030	    funext i
1031	    exact Fintype.linearIndependent_iff.mp hLIv g hg i
1032	  · -- The cokernel `T = N ⧸ range φ` is torsion: every element is killed by a
1033	    -- non-zero divisor of `P`.
1034	    intro x
1035	    obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := Submodule.Quotient.mk_surjective _ x
1036	    -- The images `ℓ (v i)` span `NK` over `K` (they are unit multiples of a basis).
1037	    have hspan : Submodule.span K (Set.range (fun i => ℓ (v i))) = ⊤ := by
1038	      refine top_unique ?_
1039	      rw [← b.span_eq, Submodule.span_le]
1040	      rintro _ ⟨i, rfl⟩
1041	      have hbi : b i = (↑(u i) : K)⁻¹ • ℓ (v i) := by
1042	        rw [hlv i, smul_smul, inv_mul_cancel₀ (u i).ne_zero, one_smul]
1043	      rw [hbi]
1044	      exact Submodule.smul_mem _ _ (Submodule.subset_span ⟨i, rfl⟩)
1045	    -- Hence `ℓ n` is a `K`-linear combination of the `ℓ (v i)`.
1046	    obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := (Submodule.mem_span_range_iff_exists_fun K).mp
1047	      (show ℓ n ∈ Submodule.span K (Set.range fun i => ℓ (v i)) by
1048	        rw [hspan]; exact Submodule.mem_top)
1049	    -- Clear the denominators of the finitely many coefficients `c i`.
1050	    obtain ⟨s, hs⟩ := IsLocalization.exist_integer_multiples S Finset.univ c
1051	    have ha : ∀ i, ∃ y : P, algebraMap P K y = (s : P) • c i := fun i =>
1052	      hs i (Finset.mem_univ i)
1053	    choose a hae using ha
1054	    -- After multiplying by `s`, `ℓ (s • n)` becomes `ℓ (φ a)`.
1055	    have key : ℓ ((s : P) • n) = ℓ (Fintype.linearCombination P v a) := by
1056	      rw [map_smul, ← hc, Fintype.linearCombination_apply, map_sum, Finset.smul_sum]
1057	      refine Finset.sum_congr rfl (fun i _ => ?_)
1058	      rw [map_smul]
1059	      -- `(s:P) • (c i • ℓ v_i) = a i • ℓ v_i`
1060	      rw [← algebraMap_smul K ((s : P)) (c i • ℓ (v i)), smul_smul, ← Algebra.smul_def,
1061	        ← hae i, algebraMap_smul]
1062	    -- So `s • n - φ a` lies in the kernel of `ℓ`, hence is killed by some `t ∈ S`.
1063	    have hker : ℓ ((s : P) • n - Fintype.linearCombination P v a) = 0 := by
1064	      rw [map_sub, key, sub_self]
1065	    obtain ⟨t, ht⟩ := (IsLocalizedModule.eq_zero_iff S ℓ).mp hker
1066	    -- The non-zero divisor `t * s` annihilates the class of `n` in `T`.
1067	    refine ⟨t * s, ?_⟩
1068	    rw [smul_sub, sub_eq_zero] at ht
1069	    have ht2 : (↑t : P) • ((↑s : P) • n) = (↑t : P) • Fintype.linearCombination P v a := ht
1070	    have hmem : (↑(t * s) : P) • n ∈ LinearMap.range (Fintype.linearCombination P v) := by
1071	      rw [Submonoid.coe_mul, mul_smul, ht2, ← map_smul]
1072	      exact LinearMap.mem_range_self _ _
1073	    -- Conclude `(t * s) • ⟦n⟧ = 0`.
1074	    rw [← Submodule.Quotient.mk_smul, Submodule.Quotient.mk_eq_zero]
1075	    exact hmem
1076	
1077	/-- **L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module**
1078	(`lem:gf_torsion_annihilator`, Nitsure §4). For `A` a noetherian domain, `d ≥ 0`,
1079	and `T` a finite *torsion* module over `P_d := A[X_1,…,X_d]`, there exists a
1080	non-zero `F ∈ Ann_{P_d}(T)`. Since `P_d` is a domain, a non-zero-divisor
1081	annihilator (provided by `Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors`) is the
1082	same thing as a non-zero one. -/
1083	theorem gf_torsion_annihilator
1084	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
1085	    (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1086	    [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1087	    (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
1088	    ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
1089	      F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T := by
1090	  obtain ⟨F, hFann, hFnzd⟩ := Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors htors
1091	  refine ⟨F, nonZeroDivisors.ne_zero hFnzd, ?_⟩
1092	  rw [← Submodule.annihilator_top]
1093	  exact hFann
1094	
1095	/-! ### L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables (`lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar`)
1096	
1097	The single-variable elimination of the support-dimension drop needs a triangular
1098	change of variables `e` making a non-zero `F ∈ A[X_0,…,X_n]` monic in `X_0` up to a
1099	unit after inverting one `g ∈ A`. Mathlib's Noether-normalisation development
1100	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.NoetherNormalization`) constructs exactly this transformation
1101	`T` and proves the leading coefficient is a unit — but only over a *field*, and as
1102	`private` lemmas. The block below replays that construction over a noetherian *domain*
1103	(the field is used in Mathlib only to conclude the top coefficient is a unit; over a
1104	domain it is merely non-zero, and becomes a unit after inverting it). The degree
1105	bookkeeping (`degreeOf_zero_t`, `leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t`, `T_leadingcoeff_eq`) is
1106	a domain-adapted transcription of the Mathlib originals. -/
1107	
1108	section NagataNormalization
1109	
1110	-- `AlgebraicGeometry.Polynomial` exists and would shadow the root namespace, so the
1111	-- `Polynomial`/`MvPolynomial` lemma APIs are opened with explicit `_root_.` prefixes.
1112	open _root_.Polynomial _root_.MvPolynomial _root_.Ideal Nat _root_.RingHom List
1113	
1114	/-- `finSuccEquiv` commutes with coefficient base change `MvPolynomial.map phi`. -/
1115	private theorem finSuccEquiv_map_comm {A B : Type*} [CommRing A] [CommRing B]
1116	    (phi : A →+* B) (m : ℕ) (q : MvPolynomial (Fin (m + 1)) A) :
1117	    finSuccEquiv B m (MvPolynomial.map phi q)
1118	      = Polynomial.map (MvPolynomial.map phi) (finSuccEquiv A m q) := by
1119	  induction q using MvPolynomial.induction_on with
1120	  | C a => simp [finSuccEquiv_apply]
1121	  | add p q hp hq => simp [hp, hq]
1122	  | mul_X p i hp =>
1123	    simp only [map_mul, Polynomial.map_mul, hp]
1124	    congr 1
1125	    cases i using Fin.cases with
1126	    | zero => simp [finSuccEquiv_X_zero]
1127	    | succ j => simp [finSuccEquiv_X_succ]
1128	
1129	variable {k : Type*} [CommRing k] [IsDomain k] {n : ℕ} (f : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) k)
1130	variable (v w : Fin (n + 1) →₀ ℕ)
1131	
1132	local notation3 "up" => 2 + f.totalDegree
1133	local notation3 "r" => fun (i : Fin (n + 1)) ↦ up ^ i.1
1134	
1135	omit [IsDomain k] in
1136	variable {f v} in
1137	private lemma lt_up (vlt : ∀ i, v i < up) : ∀ l ∈ ofFn v, l < up := by grind
1138	
1139	/-- The triangular Nagata substitution `X_i ↦ X_i + c • X_0 ^ (up ^ i)` (`i ≠ 0`),
1140	`X_0 ↦ X_0`. Transcribed from `Mathlib.RingTheory.NoetherNormalization` to a domain. -/
1141	private noncomputable abbrev T1 (c : k) :
1142	    MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) k →ₐ[k] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) k :=
1143	  aeval fun i ↦ if i = 0 then X 0 else X i + c • X 0 ^ r i
1144	
1145	omit [IsDomain k] in
1146	private lemma t1_comp_t1_neg (c : k) : (T1 f c).comp (T1 f (-c)) = AlgHom.id _ _ := by
1147	  rw [comp_aeval, ← MvPolynomial.aeval_X_left]; ext i v; cases i using Fin.cases <;> simp
1148	
1149	/-- The Nagata transformation as an `A`-algebra automorphism. -/
1150	private noncomputable abbrev T := AlgEquiv.ofAlgHom (T1 f 1) (T1 f (-1))
1151	  (t1_comp_t1_neg f 1) (by simpa using t1_comp_t1_neg f (-1))
1152	
1153	omit [IsDomain k] in
1154	private lemma sum_r_mul_ne (vlt : ∀ i, v i < up) (wlt : ∀ i, w i < up) (ne : v ≠ w) :
1155	    ∑ x : Fin (n + 1), r x * v x ≠ ∑ x : Fin (n + 1), r x * w x := by
1156	  intro h
1157	  refine ne <| Finsupp.ext <| congrFun <| ofFn_inj.mp ?_
1158	  apply ofDigits_inj_of_len_eq (Nat.lt_add_right f.totalDegree one_lt_two)
1159	    (by simp) (lt_up vlt) (lt_up wlt)
1160	  simpa only [ofDigits_eq_sum_mapIdx, mapIdx_eq_ofFn, get_ofFn, length_ofFn,
1161	    Fin.val_cast, mul_comm, sum_ofFn] using h
1162	
1163	private lemma degreeOf_zero_t {a : k} (ha : a ≠ 0) : ((T f) (monomial v a)).degreeOf 0 =
1164	    ∑ i : Fin (n + 1), (r i) * v i := by
1165	  rw [← natDegree_finSuccEquiv, monomial_eq, Finsupp.prod_pow v fun a ↦ X a]
1166	  simp only [Fin.prod_univ_succ, Fin.sum_univ_succ, map_mul, map_prod, map_pow,
1167	    AlgEquiv.ofAlgHom_apply, MvPolynomial.aeval_C, MvPolynomial.aeval_X, if_pos, Fin.succ_ne_zero,
1168	    ite_false, one_smul, map_add, finSuccEquiv_X_zero, finSuccEquiv_X_succ, algebraMap_eq]
1169	  have h (i : Fin n) :
1170	      (Polynomial.C (X (R := k) i) + Polynomial.X ^ r i.succ) ^ v i.succ ≠ 0 :=
1171	    pow_ne_zero (v i.succ) (leadingCoeff_ne_zero.mp <| by simp [add_comm, leadingCoeff_X_pow_add_C])
1172	  rw [natDegree_mul (by simp [ha]) (mul_ne_zero (by simp) (Finset.prod_ne_zero_iff.mpr
1173	    (fun i _ ↦ h i))), natDegree_mul (by simp) (Finset.prod_ne_zero_iff.mpr (fun i _ ↦ h i)),
1174	    natDegree_prod _ _ (fun i _ ↦ h i), natDegree_finSuccEquiv, degreeOf_C]
1175	  simpa only [natDegree_pow, zero_add, natDegree_X, mul_one, Fin.val_zero, pow_zero, one_mul,
1176	    add_right_inj] using Finset.sum_congr rfl (fun i _ ↦ by
1177	    rw [add_comm (Polynomial.C _), natDegree_X_pow_add_C, mul_comm])
1178	
1179	private lemma degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne (hv : v ∈ f.support) (hw : w ∈ f.support) (ne : v ≠ w) :
1180	    (T f <| monomial v <| coeff v f).degreeOf 0 ≠
1181	    (T f <| monomial w <| coeff w f).degreeOf 0 := by
1182	  rw [degreeOf_zero_t _ _ <| mem_support_iff.mp hv, degreeOf_zero_t _ _ <| mem_support_iff.mp hw]
1183	  refine sum_r_mul_ne f v w (fun i ↦ ?_) (fun i ↦ ?_) ne <;>
1184	  exact lt_of_le_of_lt ((monomial_le_degreeOf i ‹_›).trans (degreeOf_le_totalDegree f i)) (by lia)
1185	
1186	private lemma leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t :
1187	    (finSuccEquiv k n ((T f) ((monomial v) (coeff v f)))).leadingCoeff =
1188	    algebraMap k _ (coeff v f) := by
1189	  rw [monomial_eq, Finsupp.prod_fintype]
1190	  · simp only [map_mul, map_prod, leadingCoeff_mul, leadingCoeff_prod]
1191	    rw [AlgEquiv.ofAlgHom_apply, algHom_C, algebraMap_eq, finSuccEquiv_apply, eval₂Hom_C, coe_comp]
1192	    simp only [AlgEquiv.ofAlgHom_apply, Function.comp_apply, leadingCoeff_C, map_pow,
1193	      leadingCoeff_pow, algebraMap_eq]
1194	    have : ∀ j, ((finSuccEquiv k n) ((T1 f) 1 (X j))).leadingCoeff = 1 := fun j ↦ by
1195	      by_cases h : j = 0
1196	      · simp [h, finSuccEquiv_apply]
1197	      · simp only [aeval_eq_bind₁, bind₁_X_right, if_neg h, one_smul, map_add, map_pow]
1198	        obtain ⟨i, rfl⟩ := Fin.exists_succ_eq.mpr h
1199	        simp [finSuccEquiv_X_succ, finSuccEquiv_X_zero, add_comm]
1200	    simp only [this, one_pow, Finset.prod_const_one, mul_one]
1201	  exact fun i ↦ pow_zero _
1202	
1203	/-- Over a domain, the `T`-transform of a non-zero `f` has, in `X_0`, a leading
1204	coefficient equal to `C (coeff v f)` for some `v ∈ f.support` (hence non-zero). The
1205	Mathlib original concludes `IsUnit` using the field hypothesis; here we keep the exact
1206	coefficient so the caller can invert it. -/
1207	private lemma T_leadingcoeff_eq (fne : f ≠ 0) :
1208	    ∃ v ∈ f.support,
1209	      (finSuccEquiv k n (T f f)).leadingCoeff = MvPolynomial.C (coeff v f) := by
1210	  obtain ⟨v, vin, vs⟩ := Finset.exists_max_image f.support
1211	    (fun v ↦ (T f ((monomial v) (coeff v f))).degreeOf 0) (support_nonempty.mpr fne)
1212	  set h := fun w ↦ (MvPolynomial.monomial w) (coeff w f)

1241	choice is immaterial up to renaming.) -/
1242	theorem gf_nagata_monic_lastVar
1243	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (m : ℕ)
1244	    (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (m + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
1245	    ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
1246	      (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (m + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (m + 1)) A),
1247	      IsUnit
1248	        (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) m
1249	          (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff := by
1250	  obtain ⟨v, vin, hlc⟩ := T_leadingcoeff_eq F hF
1251	  have hg0 : coeff v F ≠ 0 := mem_support_iff.mp vin
1252	  refine ⟨coeff v F, hg0, T F, ?_⟩
1253	  set g := coeff v F with hg
1254	  haveI : Nontrivial (Localization.Away g) := by
1255	    have hle : Submonoid.powers g ≤ nonZeroDivisors A := by

1259	    IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit g
1260	  have hCunit : IsUnit (MvPolynomial.C (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g) g)
1261	      : MvPolynomial (Fin m) (Localization.Away g)) := hgunit.map MvPolynomial.C

1263	      (finSuccEquiv A m (T F F)).leadingCoeff ≠ 0 := by
1264	    rw [hlc, MvPolynomial.map_C]; exact hCunit.ne_zero
1265	  rw [finSuccEquiv_map_comm, leadingCoeff_map_of_leadingCoeff_ne_zero _ hne, hlc,
1266	    MvPolynomial.map_C]

1272	`rename Fin.succ`: the composite `S = R[X_1,…,X_n] → R[X_0,…,X_n] → S[X_0]` is the

1274	`optionEquivLeft` variant (`finSuccEquiv_rename_finSuccEquiv`). -/

1276	    (R : Type*) [CommRing R] (n : ℕ) (s : MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) :
1277	    MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ s) = Polynomial.C s := by

1279	  | C r => rw [MvPolynomial.rename_C, MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv_apply, MvPolynomial.eval₂Hom_C]; rfl
1280	  | add p q hp hq => simp [hp, hq]
1281	  | mul_X p i hp =>
1282	    rw [map_mul, map_mul, hp, MvPolynomial.rename_X, MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv_X_succ,

1288	univariate polynomial in `X_0` over `S := R[X_1,…,X_n]`) has a *unit* leading
1289	coefficient — i.e. `p` is monic in `X_0` up to a unit. Then `R[X_0,…,X_n]/(p)` is
1290	module-finite over `S = R[X_1,…,X_n]`, the structure being via the constant
1291	inclusion `S ↪ R[X_0,…,X_n]` (`rename Fin.succ`) followed by `Ideal.Quotient.mk`.

1293	Encoded as `RingHom.Finite` of that composite ring map (which unfolds to
1294	`Module.Finite S (R[X_0,…,X_n]/(p))` with the exotic algebra structure) to avoid an
1295	in-statement `letI` whose `Module` synthesis loops. The proof rescales `finSuccEquiv p`

1297	same ideal, invokes `Polynomial.Monic.finite_quotient`, and transports along the
1298	`S`-algebra isomorphism induced by `finSuccEquiv`. -/
1299	theorem mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_of_monic_lastVar
1300	    (R : Type*) [CommRing R] (n : ℕ)
1301	    (p : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R)
1302	    (hp : IsUnit (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p).leadingCoeff) :
1303	    ((Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {p})).comp
1304	        (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ).toRingHom :
1305	          MvPolynomial (Fin n) R →+* (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p})).Finite := by
1306	  letI algI : Algebra (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p}) :=
1307	    ((Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {p})).comp
1308	      (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ).toRingHom).toAlgebra
1309	  have hc : IsUnit ((hp.unit⁻¹ : (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)ˣ) : MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) :=
1310	    (hp.unit⁻¹).isUnit
1311	  have hcP :
1312	      IsUnit (Polynomial.C ((hp.unit⁻¹ : (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)ˣ) : MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)) :=
1313	    hc.map Polynomial.C
1314	  have hmonic : (Polynomial.C ((hp.unit⁻¹ : (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)ˣ) : MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)
1315	      * MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p).Monic := by
1316	    rw [Polynomial.Monic, Polynomial.leadingCoeff_C_mul_of_isUnit hc]
1317	    exact Units.inv_mul_of_eq hp.unit_spec
1318	  have hspan : Ideal.span {Polynomial.C ((hp.unit⁻¹ : (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)ˣ) :
1319	        MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) * MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p}
1320	      = Ideal.span {MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p} :=
1321	    Ideal.span_singleton_mul_left_unit hcP (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p)
1322	  have hfinstd : Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)
1323	      (Polynomial (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) ⧸ Ideal.span {MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p}) := by
1324	    rw [← hspan]; exact hmonic.finite_quotient
1325	  have hJ : Ideal.span {MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p}
1326	      = Ideal.map ((MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n).toRingEquiv :
1327	          MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R →+* Polynomial (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R))
1328	        (Ideal.span {p}) := by
1329	    rw [Ideal.map_span, Set.image_singleton]; rfl
1330	  let ψ : (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p})
1331	      ≃+* (Polynomial (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) ⧸ Ideal.span {MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p}) :=
1332	    Ideal.quotientEquiv (Ideal.span {p}) (Ideal.span {MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p})

1334	  have hsmul : ∀ s : MvPolynomial (Fin n) R,
1335	      ψ (algebraMap (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)
1336	          (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p}) s)
1337	      = algebraMap (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)
1338	          (Polynomial (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) ⧸ Ideal.span {MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p})
1339	          s := by
1340	    intro s
1341	    change ψ (Ideal.Quotient.mk _ (MvPolynomial.rename Fin.succ s))
1342	      = Ideal.Quotient.mk _ (Polynomial.C s)

1345	  let Ψ : (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p})
1346	      ≃ₐ[MvPolynomial (Fin n) R]
1347	      (Polynomial (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) ⧸ Ideal.span {MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p}) :=
1348	    AlgEquiv.ofRingEquiv hsmul
1349	  exact @Module.Finite.equiv (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R)
1350	    (Polynomial (MvPolynomial (Fin n) R) ⧸ Ideal.span {MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv R n p})

1354	/-- Pull back an `R`-module structure along an additive equivalence `e : M ≃+ N`, defining
1355	`r • y := e (r • e.symm y)`. With this structure `e` is `R`-linear; used in the torsion
1356	reindex to transport the reindexed module structure from the `P`-localisation of the torsion
1357	module to the goal's `A`-localisation. -/
1358	@[reducible] def pullbackModuleAddEquiv {R M N : Type*} [Semiring R] [AddCommMonoid M]
1359	    [AddCommMonoid N] [Module R M] (e : M ≃+ N) : Module R N where
1360	  smul r y := e (r • e.symm y)
1361	  one_smul y := by change e (1 • e.symm y) = y; rw [one_smul, AddEquiv.apply_symm_apply]
1362	  mul_smul r s y := by
1363	    change e ((r * s) • e.symm y) = e (r • e.symm (e (s • e.symm y)))
1364	    rw [AddEquiv.symm_apply_apply, mul_smul]
1365	  smul_zero r := by change e (r • e.symm 0) = 0; rw [map_zero, smul_zero, map_zero]
1366	  smul_add r x y := by
1367	    change e (r • e.symm (x + y)) = e (r • e.symm x) + e (r • e.symm y)
1368	    rw [map_add, smul_add, map_add]
1369	  add_smul r s y := by
1370	    change e ((r + s) • e.symm y) = e (r • e.symm y) + e (s • e.symm y)
1371	    rw [add_smul, map_add]
1372	  zero_smul y := by change e (0 • e.symm y) = 0; rw [zero_smul, map_zero]
1373	
1374	/-- Module-finiteness transports across the pulled-back structure of `pullbackModuleAddEquiv`:
1375	if `M` is a finite `R`-module then so is `N` under the pulled-back action. -/
1376	theorem finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv {R M N : Type*} [Semiring R] [AddCommMonoid M]
1377	    [AddCommMonoid N] [Module R M] [Module.Finite R M] (e : M ≃+ N) :
1378	    @Module.Finite R N _ _ (pullbackModuleAddEquiv e) := by
1379	  letI : Module R N := pullbackModuleAddEquiv e
1380	  refine Module.Finite.equiv (M := M) (N := N)
1381	    { toFun := e, map_add' := map_add e, invFun := e.symm,
1382	      left_inv := e.left_inv, right_inv := e.right_inv,
1383	      map_smul' := fun r x => ?_ }
1384	  simp only [RingHom.id_apply]
1385	  change e (r • x) = e (r • e.symm (e x))
1386	  rw [AddEquiv.symm_apply_apply]
1387	
1388	/-- A scalar tower transports across the pulled-back structures of `pullbackModuleAddEquiv`:
1389	if `e : M ≃+ N` and `M` carries compatible `Ag`- and `R`-actions (`IsScalarTower Ag R M`), then
1390	the pulled-back `Ag`- and `R`-actions on `N` form a scalar tower as well. -/
1391	theorem pullback_isScalarTower {Ag R M N : Type*} [CommSemiring Ag] [Semiring R] [Algebra Ag R]
1392	    [AddCommMonoid M] [AddCommMonoid N] [Module R M] [Module Ag M] [IsScalarTower Ag R M]
1393	    (e : M ≃+ N) :
1394	    letI := pullbackModuleAddEquiv (R := R) e
1395	    letI := pullbackModuleAddEquiv (R := Ag) e
1396	    IsScalarTower Ag R N := by
1397	  letI := pullbackModuleAddEquiv (R := R) e
1398	  letI := pullbackModuleAddEquiv (R := Ag) e
1399	  refine ⟨fun a b x => ?_⟩
1400	  change e ((a • b) • e.symm x) = e (a • e.symm (e (b • e.symm x)))
1401	  rw [AddEquiv.symm_apply_apply, smul_assoc]
1402	
1403	/-- Transport module-finiteness across a ring isomorphism of the acting ring that is
1404	compatible with given `R`-algebra structures. If `ψ : B₁ ≃+* B₂` satisfies
1405	`ψ ∘ algebraMap R B₁ = algebraMap R B₂`, `B₂` is module-finite over `R`, and `M` is
1406	module-finite over `B₁` (with the scalar tower `R → B₁ → M`), then `M` is module-finite
1407	over `R`. -/
1408	theorem finite_of_quotientRingEquiv
1409	    {R B₁ B₂ M : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing B₁] [CommRing B₂] [AddCommGroup M]
1410	    [Algebra R B₁] [Algebra R B₂] [Module B₁ M] [Module R M] [IsScalarTower R B₁ M]
1411	    (ψ : B₁ ≃+* B₂)
1412	    (hψ : ∀ r : R, ψ (algebraMap R B₁ r) = algebraMap R B₂ r)
1413	    (hB₂ : Module.Finite R B₂) (hM : Module.Finite B₁ M) :
1414	    Module.Finite R M := by
1415	  haveI := hB₂
1416	  haveI := hM
1417	  have hsymm : ∀ r : R, ψ.symm (algebraMap R B₂ r) = algebraMap R B₁ r := by
1418	    intro r; rw [← hψ r, ψ.symm_apply_apply]
1419	  haveI : Module.Finite R B₁ :=
1420	    Module.Finite.equiv (AlgEquiv.ofRingEquiv (f := ψ.symm) hsymm).toLinearEquiv
1421	  exact Module.Finite.trans B₁ M

1423	/-- Descent of a localized-module structure along a scalar tower. If `f : M →ₗ[R] M'`
1424	localises `M` at the image submonoid `S.map (algebraMap R' R)` over `R`, then its
1425	restriction of scalars to `R'` localises `M` at `S` over `R'`. -/
1426	theorem isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars
1427	    {R' R M M' : Type*} [CommRing R'] [CommRing R] [Algebra R' R]
1428	    [AddCommGroup M] [AddCommGroup M'] [Module R' M] [Module R M] [Module R' M'] [Module R M']
1429	    [IsScalarTower R' R M] [IsScalarTower R' R M']
1430	    (S : Submonoid R') (f : M →ₗ[R] M')
1431	    [IsLocalizedModule (S.map (algebraMap R' R)) f] :
1432	    IsLocalizedModule S (f.restrictScalars R') := by
1433	  apply IsLocalizedModule.mk
1434	  · intro s
1435	    rw [Module.End.isUnit_iff]
1436	    have hmem : algebraMap R' R (s : R') ∈ S.map (algebraMap R' R) := ⟨s, s.2, rfl⟩
1437	    have hu := IsLocalizedModule.map_units f ⟨algebraMap R' R (s : R'), hmem⟩
1438	    rw [Module.End.isUnit_iff] at hu
1439	    have hfun : ⇑((algebraMap R' (Module.End R' M')) (s : R'))
1440	        = ⇑((algebraMap R (Module.End R M')) (algebraMap R' R (s : R'))) := by
1441	      funext x
1442	      rw [Module.algebraMap_end_apply, Module.algebraMap_end_apply, IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul]
1443	    rw [hfun]
1444	    exact hu
1445	  · intro y
1446	    obtain ⟨⟨t, sm⟩, hsm⟩ := IsLocalizedModule.surj (S.map (algebraMap R' R)) f y
1447	    obtain ⟨s, hsS, hs⟩ := sm.2
1448	    refine ⟨⟨t, ⟨s, hsS⟩⟩, ?_⟩
1449	    change (s : R') • y = (f.restrictScalars R') t
1450	    rw [LinearMap.restrictScalars_apply, ← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul R (s : R') y, hs]
1451	    exact hsm
1452	  · intro x₁ x₂ h
1453	    rw [LinearMap.restrictScalars_apply, LinearMap.restrictScalars_apply] at h
1454	    obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := IsLocalizedModule.exists_of_eq (S := S.map (algebraMap R' R)) (f := f) h
1455	    obtain ⟨s, hsS, hs⟩ := c.2
1456	    refine ⟨⟨s, hsS⟩, ?_⟩
1457	    change (s : R') • x₁ = (s : R') • x₂
1458	    rw [← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul R (s : R') x₁,
1459	        ← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul R (s : R') x₂, hs]
1460	    exact hc

1472	over `A_g[X_1,…,X_{m'}]` (one may take `m' = d - 1`). -/
1473	theorem gf_torsion_reindex
1474	    (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
1475	    (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
1476	    [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T] [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1477	    [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1478	    (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
1479	    ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
1480	      (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1481	              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
1482	      (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
1483	              (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1484	              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
1485	      Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1486	        (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T) := by
1487	  -- Reshape `d = m + 1` (uses `hd : 0 < d`); the distinguished eliminated variable is `X₀`.
1488	  obtain ⟨m, rfl⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_succ_of_ne_zero hd.ne'
1489	  -- L5b.1 (`gf_torsion_annihilator`): a non-zero annihilator `F` of the torsion module `T`.
1490	  obtain ⟨F, hF0, hFann⟩ := gf_torsion_annihilator A (m + 1) T htors
1491	  -- L5b.2 (`gf_nagata_monic_lastVar`): a triangular `A`-algebra automorphism `e` and a
1492	  -- non-zero `g ∈ A` making `e F` monic in `X₀` up to a unit after inverting `g`.
1493	  obtain ⟨g, hg0, e, hunit⟩ := gf_nagata_monic_lastVar A m F hF0

1495	  -- `A_g[X₁,…,X_m] = MvPolynomial (Fin m) A_g` has `A_g[X₀,…,X_m]/(map (e F))` module-finite
1496	  -- over it; this is the source of the dimension drop `m' = m < m + 1`.
1497	  have hfin := mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_of_monic_lastVar (Localization.Away g) m
1498	    (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F)) hunit
1499	  classical
1500	  -- ASSEMBLY. Strategy: work with the *`P`-localisation* `Tg' := LocalizedModule (C(powers g)) T`

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57	
58	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
59	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
60	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
61	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
62	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
63	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
64	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
65	% there exists a finite filtration
66	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
67	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
68	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
69	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
70	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
74	\begin{proof}
75	
76	
77	\leanok
78	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
79	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
80	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
81	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
82	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
83	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
84	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
85	
86	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
87	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
88	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
89	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
90	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
91	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
92	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
93	  for exactly this residue.
94	
95	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
96	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
97	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
98	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
99	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
100	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
101	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
102	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
103	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
104	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
105	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
106	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
107	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
108	\end{proof}
109	
110	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
111	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
112	
113	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
114	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
115	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
116	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
117	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
118	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
119	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
120	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
121	
122	\begin{lemma}\leanok
123	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
124	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
125	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
126	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
127	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
128	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
129	\end{lemma}
130	
131	\begin{proof}
132	
133	
134	\leanok
135	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
136	  finitely presented, so
137	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
138	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
139	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
140	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
141	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
142	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
143	  \(f := r\).
144	\end{proof}
145	
146	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
147	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
148	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
149	  \mathlibok
150	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
151	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
152	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
153	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
154	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
155	\end{lemma}
156	
157	\begin{lemma}\leanok
158	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
159	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
160	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
161	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
162	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
164	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
165	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
166	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
167	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
168	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
169	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
170	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
171	  induction).}
172	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
173	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
174	  \(A_f\).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	
180	\leanok
181	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
182	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
183	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
184	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
185	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
186	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
187	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
188	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
189	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
190	\end{proof}
191	
192	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
193	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
194	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
195	  \mathlibok
196	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
197	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
198	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
199	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
200	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
201	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
202	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
203	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
204	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
205	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
206	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
207	\end{lemma}

209	\begin{lemma}\leanok
210	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
211	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
213	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
214	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
215	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
216	  exact sequence
217	  \[
218	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
219	  \]
220	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
221	\end{lemma}
222	
223	\begin{proof}
224	
225	
226	\leanok
227	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
228	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
229	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence.
230	\end{proof}
231	
232	\begin{lemma}\leanok
233	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
234	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
236	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
237	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
238	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
239	\end{lemma}
240	
241	\begin{proof}
242	
243	
244	\leanok
245	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
246	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
247	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
248	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
249	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
250	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
251	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
252	\end{proof}
253	
254	\begin{lemma}\leanok
255	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
256	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
257	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
258	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
259	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
260	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
261	\end{lemma}
262	
263	\begin{proof}
264	
265	
266	\leanok
267	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
268	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
269	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{lemma}\leanok
273	[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
274	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
275	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
276	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
277	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
278	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
279	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
281	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
282	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
283	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
284	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
285	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
286	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
287	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."
288	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
289	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
290	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
291	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
292	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
293	\end{lemma}
294	
295	\begin{proof}
296	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
297	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
298	        \leanok
299	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
300	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
301	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
302	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
303	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
304	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
305	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
306	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
307	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
308	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
309	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
310	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
311	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
312	  free over \(A_f\).

315	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
316	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
317	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
318	  \mathlibok
319	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
320	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
321	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
322	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
323	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
324	\end{lemma}
325	
326	\begin{lemma}
327	
328	
329	\leanok
330	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
331	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
333	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
334	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
335	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
336	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
337	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
338	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
339	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
340	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
341	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
342	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
343	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
344	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
345	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
346	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
347	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
348	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
349	\end{lemma}
350	
351	\begin{proof}
352	
353	\leanok
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
355	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
356	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
357	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
358	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
359	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
360	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
361	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
362	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
363	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
364	\end{proof}
365	
366	\begin{lemma}\leanok
367	[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
368	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
370	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator,
371	        lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
372	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
373	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
374	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
375	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
376	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
377	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
378	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
379	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
380	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
381	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
382	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
383	  normalisation with denominators).}
384	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
385	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
386	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
387	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
388	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
389	  \[
390	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
391	  \]
392	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
393	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
394	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
395	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
396	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
397	\end{lemma}
398	
399	\begin{proof}
400	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
401	  \leanok
402	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
403	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
404	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
405	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
406	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
407	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
408	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
409	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
410	
411	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
412	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
413	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
414	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
415	  \(x \in \sigma\), a standard integrality-and-localization descent argument yields a
416	  non-zero \(a_x \in A\) such that \(a_x \cdot (1 \otimes x)\)
417	  is integral over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
418	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
419	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
420	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
421	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
422	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
423	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the proof discharges a whole generator
424	  at once using this integrality-descent step, folding that primitive over the coefficients
425	  internally.)
426	
427	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
428	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
429	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
430	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
431	
432	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
433	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
434	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
435	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
436	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
437	  \[
438	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
439	  \]
440	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
441	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
442	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
443	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
444	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
445	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
446	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
447	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
448	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
449	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
450	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
451	
452	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
453	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
454	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
455	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
456	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
457	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
458	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
459	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
460	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
461	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
462	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
463	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
464	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
465	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
466	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
467	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
468	
469	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
470	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
471	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
472	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
473	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
474	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
475	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
476	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
477	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
478	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
479	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
480	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
481	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
482	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
483	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
484	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
485	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
486	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
487	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
488	\end{proof}
489	
490	\begin{lemma}\leanok
491	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
492	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
493	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
494	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
495	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
496	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
497	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
498	\end{lemma}
499	\begin{proof}
500	
501	\leanok
502	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
503	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
504	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
505	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
506	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
507	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
508	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
509	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
510	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
511	\end{proof}
512	
513	\begin{lemma}
514	
515	
516	\leanok
517	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
518	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
519	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
520	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
521	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
522	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
523	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
524	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
525	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
526	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
527	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
528	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
529	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
530	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
531	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
532	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
533	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
534	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
535	  \[
536	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
537	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
538	  \]
539	
540	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
541	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
542	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
543	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
544	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
545	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
546	\end{lemma}
547	
548	\begin{proof}
549	
550	\leanok
551	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
552	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
553	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
554	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
555	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
556	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
557	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
558	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
559	
560	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
561	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
562	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
563	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
564	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
565	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
566	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
567	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
568	
569	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
570	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
571	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
572	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
573	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
574	\end{proof}
575	
576	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
577	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
578	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
579	  \mathlibok
580	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
581	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
582	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
583	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
584	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
585	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
586	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
587	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
588	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
589	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
590	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
591	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
592	\end{lemma}
593	
594	\begin{lemma}\leanok
595	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
596	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
597	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
598	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
599	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
600	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
601	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
602	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
603	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
604	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
605	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
606	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
607	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
608	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
609	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
610	\end{lemma}
611	
612	\begin{proof}
613	
614	\leanok
615	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
616	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
617	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
618	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
619	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
620	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
621	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
622	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
623	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
624	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
625	\end{proof}

627	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
628	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
629	
630	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
631	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
632	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
633	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
634	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
635	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
636	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
637	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
638	
639	\begin{definition}\leanok
640	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
641	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
642	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
643	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
644	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
645	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
646	  \[
647	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
648	  \]
649	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
650	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
651	\end{definition}
652	
653	\begin{lemma}\leanok
654	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
655	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
656	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
657	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
658	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
659	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
660	\end{lemma}
661	\begin{proof}
662	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
663	  \leanok
664	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
665	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
666	\end{proof}
667	
668	\begin{definition}\leanok
669	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
670	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
671	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
672	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
673	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
674	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
675	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
676	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
677	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
678	\end{definition}
679	
680	\begin{lemma}\leanok
681	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
682	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
683	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
684	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
685	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
686	\end{lemma}
687	\begin{proof}
688	
689	\leanok
690	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
691	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
692	\end{proof}
693	
694	\begin{lemma}\leanok
695	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
696	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
697	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
698	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
699	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
700	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
701	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
702	\end{lemma}
703	\begin{proof}
704	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
705	  \leanok
706	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
707	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
708	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
709	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
710	\end{proof}
711	
712	\begin{lemma}\leanok
713	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
714	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
715	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
716	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
717	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
718	  transformed monomial is the radix value
719	  \[
720	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
721	  \]
722	\end{lemma}
723	\begin{proof}
724	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
725	  \leanok
726	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
727	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
728	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
729	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
730	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
731	\end{proof}
732	
733	\begin{lemma}\leanok
734	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
735	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
736	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
737	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
738	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
739	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
740	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
741	\end{lemma}
742	\begin{proof}
743	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
744	  \leanok
745	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
746	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
747	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
748	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
749	\end{proof}
750	
751	\begin{lemma}\leanok
752	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
753	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
754	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
755	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
756	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
757	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
758	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
759	\end{lemma}
760	\begin{proof}
761	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
762	  \leanok
763	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
764	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
765	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
766	\end{proof}
767	
768	\begin{lemma}\leanok
769	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
770	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
772	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
773	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
774	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
775	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
776	\end{lemma}
777	\begin{proof}
778	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
779	  \leanok
780	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
781	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
782	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
783	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
784	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
785	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
786	\end{proof}
787	
788	\begin{lemma}\leanok
789	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
790	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
791	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
792	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
793	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
794	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
795	\end{lemma}
796	\begin{proof}
797	
798	\leanok
799	  By induction on \(q\): the isomorphism is additive and multiplicative, and agrees with
800	  \(\varphi\) on constants and variables, so it commutes with \(\varphi\) on all polynomials.
801	\end{proof}
802	
803	\begin{lemma}\leanok
804	[Constant inclusion under the variable-splitting isomorphism]
805	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
807	  For \(s \in R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), the composite of the inclusion
808	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) (sending \(X_j \mapsto X_j\))
809	  with the isomorphism \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) sends \(s\)
810	  to the constant polynomial \(s \in (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\).
811	\end{lemma}
812	\begin{proof}
813	
814	\leanok
815	  By induction on \(s\): a constant and each variable \(X_j\) (\(j \ge 1\)) map to themselves,
816	  and the isomorphism is an algebra map, so it sends products and sums of such to constants in
817	  the outer polynomial ring.
818	\end{proof}
819	
820	\begin{lemma}\leanok
821	[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
822	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
824	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
825	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
826	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
827	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
828	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
829	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
830	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
831	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
832	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
833	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
834	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
835	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
836	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
837	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
838	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
839	\end{lemma}
840	
841	\begin{proof}
842	
843	\leanok
844	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
845	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
846	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
847	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
848	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
849	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
850	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
851	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
852	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
853	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
854	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
855	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
856	\end{proof}
857	
858	\begin{lemma}[L5b engine anchor — quotient by a monic univariate polynomial is finite]
859	  \label{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite}
860	  \lean{Polynomial.Monic.finite_quotient}
861	  \mathlibok
862	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
863	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in S[X]\) a monic polynomial. Then the quotient
864	  \(S[X]/(f)\) is a module-finite \(S\)-module (indeed free of rank \(\deg f\)).
865	\end{lemma}
866	
867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
868	[L5b.3 — single-variable elimination engine (shared)]
869	  \label{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_of_monic_lastVar}
871	  \uses{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
872	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
873	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
874	  % the division algorithm in one variable is the standard mechanism of the support drop.
875	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
876	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
877	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
878	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
879	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
880	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
881	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
882	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
883	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
884	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
885	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
886	\end{lemma}
887	
888	\begin{proof}
